content.full_text:
el California

Memorandum

To : Opinion Request File                                    Date : FEB. 4, 1982

                                                             M1-82-99

From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
       Steve Crooks

Subject: Disqualification of a Former Coastal Commission Employee
         Under Government Code Sections 87400 - 87405 (Revolving Door)

         The above subject was presented for consideration at the
January 21, 1982, Opinion Request Meeting. The central issue of the topic's discussion was whether or not a new permit application before the Coastal Commission is the "same proceeding" as that of a previous permit application for the same development site. (See Government Code Section 87400 (c)). The conclusion of the discussion was that the new permit application would be part of the former permit proceeding depending on how much of a permit analyst's work on the previous permit would be relied on by the Coastal Commission in reviewing the new permit application. Therefore, it was agreed that I should contact the Coastal Commission staff for their comments on this question.

SC:er

State of California                                [seal]

Memorandum

To    : Opinion Request Meeting Participants                Date : Jan. 12, 1982


From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
       Steven Crooks    [signature]

Subject: Disqualification of a Former Coastal Commission Employee
         under Government Code Sections 87400 - 87405 (Revolving Door)


     The Coastal Commission (CC) has referred a case to us
involving possible violations of Government Code Sections
87401 and 87402. There are two different scenarios involving
the same former employee. Thus, there could be one or more
violations for each set of facts.

SCENARIO NUMBER ONE

     While a CC staff member, the former employee invest-
igated a possible violation of a CC construction permit.
He visited the construction site and observed what he
initially believed could be a permit violation in the con-
struction of a house. He was informed by the contractor
at the site, however, that the issued permit was written
such that the apparent violation was actually a permitted
construction feature in this case. Upon returning to the
CC office, the former employee checked the permit issued
for that project and confirmed the contractor's statement
as to the permit allowing for this supposed violation. The
former employee then contacted the contractor and informed
him there did not appear to be a violation.

     After leaving the CC staff, the former employee was
contacted by the owner of this same house. The owner
first had tried to contact the former employee at the CC
office. Since the former employee no longer worked there,
the owner obtained the former employee's home telephone
number from the CC staff. The owner contacted the former
employee asking him to check on different violations at
the owner's house still under construction. The former
employee agreed; he again reviewed the CC permit and found
discrepancies between the actual construction and the design
called for in the permit. The former employee relayed
this finding to the owner and suggested filing an immaterial
amendment to correct the discrepancies. A CC staff member
was contacted briefly by the former employee about obtaining
an immaterial amendment in this case. However, the former
employee did not file for an amendment or further assist
the owner in obtaining an amendment. The owner later

Opinion Request Meeting
January 12, 1932
Page two


obtained the amendment on his own. The former employee
received no compensation for his efforts as the owner was
a "friend of a friend of a friend."

SCENARIO NUMBER TWO

While a CC staff member, the former employee was the
permit analyst for a particular Malibu-area development
project. (This project is in no way connected to the house
and owner in Scenario Number One.) He wrote a permit re-
commendation for approval of the project. The recommendation
includes three preliminary conditions the developer has to
meet in order for the permit to be effective. The first
condition requires the developer to participate in a land
retirement program. This condition could be satisfied
either by filing a certain number of Transfer of Development
Credits (TDCs) with the Commission, or by paying fees to
the Coastal Conservancy who may then purchase the TDCs
from someone else. The second and third conditions involve
dedicating a recreational easement to the Santa Monica
Mountain Restoration Trust.

This permit recommendation may be contrasted with a
previous recommendation of denial for this particular project
by a different CC permit analyst. Moreover, when the conditional
permit was granted, the developer still did not receive
approval for the entire number of residences he wished to
build; the tentative county zoning map for the area did not
allow the density of residences which the developer wanted to
build.

After leaving the CC staff, the former employee was
sought out by the same developer. Not finding him on the
CC staff, the developer located the former employee by
searching through telephone book. The developer wanted
the advice of the former employee because the former employee
is the one person most familiar with all the facets of the
developer's project in the context of CC permit and develop-
ment requirements. Since the time the conditional permit
was issued, the county zoning map for the project area has
been modified in its final form; the developer may now
build to the density he originally desired.

In a meeting between the developer, the former employee
and a private land development consultant (on contract with
the Coastal Conservancy), the possibility of seeking an
amendment to the conditions of the permit was discussed.
The former employee said he was not sure he could do any-
thing for the developer because of the restrictions in
Government Code sections 87410 and 87402. Nevertheless,
the former employee apparently determined he could preliminin-
arily advise and represent the developer with in the language
of those sections, because the former employee received

Opinion Request Meeting
January 12, 1982
Page three

no compensation. He told the developer he would make
an initial investigation for him on the feasibility of
either getting an amendment to the permit's conditions
or seeking an entirely new permit for a redesigned higher
density project on the same site. The amendment proposal
would allow the TDC condition to be satisfied by dedicating
as part of the recreational easement, that amount of land
which the developer previously was prohibited from developing
because of the former zoning plan. The private land consultant
advised that this amendment would not be accepted by the
Commission based on past CC practice. However, a present
CC staff member was contacted by the former employee as to
what the former employee's understanding of the permit's
conditions were in the context of such an amendment. The
CC staff member said he disagreed with the former employee's
interpretation of the permit's conditions. The former
employee now contends that his investigation was really
focused on the possibility of an entirely new project and
permit as he determined that an amendment to the original
permit would be "too complex." The developer says that
he just wants to go ahead with the project as originally
approved by the Commission, but is waiting for the formation
of a new local non-profit trust to whom to dedicate the
recreational easement.

SC:er
